metadata.summary: Embed accessibility at design stage: contrast ratios, semantic structure, touch targets, motion controls, focus management. 70-80% of defects are preventable at design time.

content.title: Accessibility-First Design
content.summary: Methodology for embedding accessibility requirements at the design stage — color contrast ratios, semantic structure, touch target sizing, motion controls, and focus management — before a single line of code is written. The core rule: 70-80% of accessibility defects are preventable at design time; retrofitting costs far more than getting it right first.

why.p: Accessibility defects found in development cost 10-100x more to fix than those caught in design. Designing with WCAG AA contrast ratios, semantic heading hierarchies, visible focus states, and 44×44px touch targets removes entire categories of defects. It also expands the addressable user base and satisfies legal compliance requirements in the EU, US (ADA), and UK.

list.item: Starting a new UI design (web, mobile, or desktop)
Adding a new component or page to an existing design system
Conducting a design review before dev handoff
Evaluating a Figma/Sketch file for accessibility gaps
Writing design spec annotations for developers
Post-production audits where design files no longer exist — use code-level audit tools (axe, Lighthouse) instead
Back-end or API work with no visual output
Content writing (separate content accessibility concerns apply)
Late-stage retrofit of an existing product — use a11y-testing to find issues, then fix systematically

section.name: color-and-contrast
section.p: Color and contrast are the foundation of visual accessibility. These rules ensure text and UI components are readable for low-vision users and in various lighting conditions.

rule.statement: Body text must meet a 4.5:1 contrast ratio against its background (WCAG AA). Large text (18pt+ or 14pt bold) requires 3:1. UI components and focus indicators require 3:1.
rule.rationale: Contrast ratios below these thresholds make text unreadable for low-vision users and in high-ambient-light environments.

rule.statement: Never use color as the sole indicator of meaning (e.g., red = error). Always pair color with a text label, icon, or pattern.
rule.rationale: Approximately 8% of males have color vision deficiency; color-only signals are invisible to them.

rule.statement: Test designs with a color blindness simulator (e.g., Stark plugin) for deuteranopia, protanopia, and achromatopsia before developer handoff.
rule.rationale: Simulation catches color-only patterns that pass contrast ratio checks but still fail color-blind users.

section.name: touch-targets-and-spacing
section.p: Touch targets and spacing directly impact usability for users with motor impairments. These precise rules prevent mis-taps and interaction failures.

rule.statement: Touch targets must be at minimum 24×24px (WCAG 2.2 AA). Use 44×44px as the production target; 48×48dp for Material Design contexts.
rule.rationale: Targets below 24px are consistently missed by users with motor impairments and elderly users with reduced dexterity.

rule.statement: Maintain at least 8px spacing between adjacent interactive elements to prevent mis-taps.
rule.rationale: Dense interactive element layouts produce high error rates for users with tremors or limited fine motor control.

section.name: focus-and-keyboard-navigation
section.p: Keyboard navigation and focus management are critical for users who cannot use a mouse. These rules ensure full keyboard accessibility.

rule.statement: Design visible focus indicators for every interactive element with a minimum 3:1 contrast ratio against adjacent colors. Never remove the default browser focus ring without a replacement.
rule.rationale: Keyboard-only users and switch control users navigate entirely via focus indicators; invisible focus makes the interface unusable.

rule.statement: Plan and annotate the keyboard focus order in design specs. Logical tab order must follow reading order; modal dialogs must trap focus while open and return focus to the trigger on close.
rule.rationale: An unspecified focus order results in developer-guessed tab sequences that are often illogical or inaccessible.

section.name: motion-and-time-limits
section.p: Motion and time limits have serious implications for certain user populations. These rules protect vestibular and photosensitive users.

rule.statement: All animations must be pausable and must respect the prefers-reduced-motion media query. No auto-playing video with sound. No flashing content above 3 flashes per second.
rule.rationale: Motion triggers vestibular disorders in some users; flashing above 3 Hz can induce photosensitive seizures.

rule.statement: Remove time limits from interactive flows, or make them adjustable and extendable with at least 20 seconds warning.
rule.rationale: Fixed time limits exclude slow readers, users with cognitive disabilities, and screen reader users who process content sequentially.

section.name: semantic-structure
section.p: Semantic structure is how assistive technologies understand content hierarchy and relationships. These rules ensure screen readers and other AT can navigate effectively.

rule.statement: Specify heading levels (H1 → H2 → H3) in design annotations. Never skip a heading level. Every page must have exactly one H1.
rule.rationale: Screen reader users navigate by heading; a broken hierarchy makes content structure incomprehensible.

rule.statement: All form inputs must have visible labels connected via for/id. Placeholder text is not a label substitute — it disappears on input and has insufficient contrast.
rule.rationale: Placeholder-only labels fail blind users who cannot see the placeholder and sighted users once they start typing.

rule.statement: Include in design handoff: heading levels, image alt text, link text (no "click here"), focus order, ARIA labels for custom components, error message copy, loading state announcements.
rule.rationale: Developers cannot infer accessibility semantics from visual designs; explicit annotation is required for correct implementation.

section.name: semantic-html-vs-div-soup
section.p: Semantic HTML provides free accessibility structure that cannot be replicated with divs and CSS alone.

example.code:
• Home
  • About

Main Title

Section Title

Content here...

Related Articles
example.note: Native landmark elements (nav, main, aside, footer) give screen readers free navigation without extra ARIA.

antipattern.description: Using only div and span with CSS classes for all layout and interactive elements.
antipattern.reason: Screen readers receive no semantic information; keyboard navigation and assistive technology integrations break entirely.

section.name: forms
section.p: Form accessibility is critical. These patterns ensure all users can identify, understand, and complete form interactions.

rule.statement: Every input must have a visible label connected via for/id. Use aria-describedby for helper text. Mark required fields with both an asterisk and aria-required. Group related inputs with fieldset + legend.
rule.rationale: Screen readers announce labels on focus; without a programmatic label the field is announced only by type ("edit blank"), giving no context.

rule.statement: Error messages must use aria-invalid="true" on the input and role="alert" on the error text so screen readers announce errors without requiring the user to navigate to them.
rule.rationale: Sighted users see inline errors visually; blind users only discover errors if they are programmatically announced.

example.code:
We'll only use this to send your receipt.

Enter a valid email address.

section.name: modal-dialogs
section.p: Modal dialogs require special focus and keyboard handling to remain accessible.

rule.statement: When a modal opens: move focus to the first interactive element inside it, trap Tab/Shift+Tab within it, close on Escape key, and return focus to the trigger element on close.
rule.rationale: Without focus trapping, keyboard users tab out of the modal into an obscured background; without Escape support they cannot exit without a mouse.

rule.statement: Mark background content with aria-hidden="true" when a modal is open so screen readers do not read background content.
rule.rationale: Without aria-hidden, screen reader users can navigate the background page while the modal is open, causing confusion.

section.name: buttons-vs-links
section.p: Buttons and links are semantically different and must be used correctly for assistive technology compatibility.

rule.statement: Use button for actions that do not change the URL (submit, toggle, open modal, delete). Use anchor (a) for navigation to a new page or section. Never style one as the other without role correction.
rule.rationale: Screen readers and voice control software identify interactive elements by role; a div styled as a button is not keyboard-reachable and not announced as a button.

section.name: skip-links
section.p: Skip links allow keyboard users to bypass repetitive navigation content.

rule.statement: Place a "Skip to main content" link as the first focusable element on every page. It may be visually hidden until focused.
rule.rationale: Keyboard users and screen reader users must Tab through all navigation on every page load without a skip link — a severe usability barrier on navigation-heavy pages.

section.name: progressive-enhancement
section.p: Progressive enhancement ensures core functionality remains accessible even when JavaScript is unavailable or fails.

rule.statement: Build in layers: (1) semantic HTML providing core functionality, (2) CSS for visual presentation, (3) JavaScript for enhanced interactions. Core functionality must work without JavaScript.
rule.rationale: JS-only interfaces break for users on low-bandwidth connections, corporate environments that block JS, and assistive technologies that execute JS inconsistently.

section.name: mobile-checklist
section.p: Mobile accessibility extends desktop principles with touch-specific considerations.

list.item: Touch targets at minimum 44x44px on all interactive elements
No user-scalable=no in viewport meta — pinch zoom must be enabled
Text resizes to 200% without horizontal scroll (WCAG 1.4.4)
Forms compatible with platform autofill (correct input type and autocomplete attributes)
Both portrait and landscape orientation supported

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Agents help most at handoff: convert design specs to a11y annotations (heading levels, ARIA labels, focus order, alt text drafts), check tokens (contrast, sizing) against WCAG, generate semantic HTML scaffolds from a wireframe description, audit error/empty/loading states. Agents are weak at: judging visual hierarchy, evaluating motion comfort, weighing brand-vs-a11y tradeoffs. Pipeline: design tokens → contrast/size linter → component scaffold (semantic HTML) → axe regression in Storybook.
Recommended subagents:

list.item: faion-sdd-executor-agent — gate component "done" on a11y checklist conformance + Storybook axe pass.
Token-audit subagent — given color tokens, output the full pairwise WCAG contrast matrix and flag failing pairs by usage.
Component-scaffold subagent — given a Figma component description, produce semantic HTML + minimal ARIA skeleton.
Annotation-writer subagent — convert Figma layer names + comments into A11y Annotation Kit format for dev handoff.
See also: a11y-testing, wcag-22-compliance, cognitive-inclusion-design.

section.name: prompt-patterns
section.p: These prompt patterns guide agents in accessibility tasks:

example.title: Contrast matrix audit
example.code: Audit this design-system color token JSON. For each FG/BG pairing actually used in components, compute the WCAG ratio and flag: - <4.5 for body text → FAIL. - <3.0 for non-text UI / focus → FAIL. - <7.0 if AAA target → WARN. Return a markdown table sorted by ratio asc.

example.title: Semantic HTML scaffold
example.code: Convert this wireframe description to semantic HTML scaffold: - Choose correct landmark elements (header/nav/main/aside/footer). - Heading hierarchy. - Buttons vs. links per behavior. - Form labels with for/id. - Required ARIA only (no overuse). Return HTML + a list of ARIA labels for the dev to localize.

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: Be aware of these common pitfalls when using agents for accessibility work:

antipattern.description: Agents over-add ARIA roles (e.g., "role=button" on actual button elements).
antipattern.reason: Enforce "no ARIA when native exists" rule in prompts.

antipattern.description: LLM-generated alt text invents content for unfamiliar imagery.
antipattern.reason: Supply caption/context or require human approval for non-decorative images.

antipattern.description: Heading-hierarchy guesses miss visual context (an H1-styled subtitle is not an H1).
antipattern.reason: Require designer-confirmed heading structure as input.

antipattern.description: Agent skip-link suggestions can collide with framework routers (anchor + SPA route).
Color suggestions to "fix contrast" can break brand palette.
antipattern.reason: Agents should propose tonal scale extensions, not arbitrary hex changes.

antipattern.description: Auto-applied prefers-reduced-motion can hide essential CSS-driven loaders.
antipattern.reason: Provide static fallback, not just "remove animation".

antipattern.description: Agents tend to satisfy axe but miss focus visibility on dark themes.
antipattern.reason: Require visual regression with Chromatic or Percy + a11y addon.

antipattern.description: "Use ARIA labels everywhere" is not the same as accessibility.
antipattern.reason: Redundant labels confuse screen readers (announces twice). Agent must check pairing.

list.item: Bake contrast checks into the design-system token build pipeline; fail CI when a token pair drops below threshold for its declared use.
Write a11y acceptance criteria into every component spec (focus states, keyboard, ARIA, touch target).
Use semantic HTML first; reach for ARIA only when no native equivalent exists. Rule: "no ARIA is better than bad ARIA".
Define focus order in design (numbered overlay) before dev handoff.
Annotate every image at design time — alt text either inline or "decorative" tag.
Touch targets: 24×24 px is WCAG 2.2 minimum; 44×44 px is platform recommendation. Use 44 for all primary actions.
Run eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y + Storybook a11y addon as merge-blocking CI.
Pair with cognitive-inclusion-design when designing forms, errors, and learning content.
pa11y / pa11y-ci — Static page scanning during dev. Install: npm i -g pa11y
@axe-core/cli — Headless axe. Install: npm i -g @axe-core/cli
storybook-addon-a11y — a11y panel in Storybook. https://storybook.js.org/addons/@storybook/addon-a11y
eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y — Lint JSX for a11y issues. Install: npm i -D eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y
axe-playwright / cypress-axe / jest-axe — Component-level a11y assertions. Install: npm i -D
Figma plugin: A11y Annotation Kit — Annotation handoff. https://www.figma.com/community/plugin/731310036968334777
Stark (Figma/Sketch/XD) — Contrast, color blindness, focus. https://www.getstark.co
tonik / polaris-tokens style-dictionary — Token build with contrast checks. https://amzn.github.io/style-dictionary/

section.name: services-and-apps
section.p: Various services and apps support accessibility work at different stages:

list.item: Stark (SaaS / plugin) — Best design-stage tooling; team plans for handoff.
Figma + a11y plugins (A11y Annotation Kit, Contrast, Able) — Manual plugin use during design crit.
Chromatic + a11y addon (SaaS) — Visual regression + a11y per Storybook story. API/CI integration.
Token Studio (Figma) (SaaS / plugin) — Manage tokens with contrast metadata.
Specify / zeroheight (SaaS) — Design-system docs with a11y notes per component. API integration.
Material Design / Apple HIG / Fluent — Platform-native baselines. Documentation reference.
Microsoft Inclusive Design Toolkit — Persona spectrum exercises. Documentation reference.

section.name: contrast-matrix-template
section.p: Inline contrast-matrix generator from style-dictionary tokens. This Python script computes WCAG contrast ratios for all color pairings:

section.name: when-to-use-additional
section.p: Additional context for when this methodology applies specifically:

list.item: New product / new feature design — apply at wireframe + visual-design stage to avoid 70-80% of post-launch a11y debt.
Design-system foundation work (colors, typography, focus states, spacing tokens).
Component library being built — bake a11y into each primitive's API and stories.
Brand refresh / theme rework — ensure new palette passes contrast at design time, not at QA.
Onboarding new designers / dev to a project — use the checklist as standards baseline.

section.name: limitations
section.p: Design-time accessibility checks have important limitations:

list.item: Design-time checks (contrast, target size) catch only ~50-60% of issues; runtime issues (ARIA misuse, keyboard traps) need code-level audits.
Designers using Figma/Sketch can't test screen-reader behavior — only previews. Real AT testing happens post-handoff.
Touch-target rules conflict with information-density goals (data tables, pro tools); needs case-by-case judgment.
"Inclusive design principles" are guidelines, not pass/fail; agents struggle to apply them without context.
Progressive enhancement only works if the team commits to it; partial buy-in produces JS-only UIs that fail without scripts.
